title: Making a book cradle, a support for reading fragile bindings
subtitle: A simple foam or board cradle that holds a book open without stressing the spine
excerpt: A book cradle holds old and fragile volumes open at a safe angle without forcing the spine flat. It is a standard tool in libraries and archives. A working version takes ten minutes to cut from foam board or corrugated card.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on the care of books: advice on supporting volumes when open to avoid hinge damage. The foam-board cradle construction is the contemporary conservation standard, derived from Cockerell's principles and adapted for home use with readily available materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: The angle at which a book's covers are held when open. Fragile bindings should not be opened past 90°; tight or damaged spines may need cradles angled at 60° or 45° to prevent cracking.

content.text: Old books crack at the hinges when forced fully open. The hinge is the most mechanically stressed part of a binding, every degree past the safe
puts more load on the leather or cloth at the joint. A cradle supports both covers and the spine in a fixed position, distributing the weight of the boards away from the hinge.

attrs.title: Cutting tool safety
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Always cut on a cutting mat against a metal ruler. Keep the non-cutting hand behind the blade's path.

items.name: Foam board, 5 mm (two sheets A3)
items.notes: Foam board, polystyrene foam faced on both sides with card, cuts cleanly and is light. Two A3 sheets are sufficient for a standard book cradle. Corrugated card works as an alternative but is less rigid.

items.name: Craft knife
items.notes: With a fresh blade. Foam board requires firm, clean cuts.

items.name: Cutting mat, A4
items.notes: For cutting the foam board pieces.

content.text: A cradle has two angled ramps joined at a low point. Measure the book: height, and the combined width of both open covers. Cut two ramp pieces from the foam board, each the full height of the book and approximately 30-40 mm wide at the top, tapering to 10 mm at the base, with the slope angle set to match the intended opening angle, 60° for most fragile bindings, 90° for books in good condition. Cut a base strip 20 mm wide and the full height of the book to space the two ramps apart.

Stand the two ramps facing each other with the base strip between them at the lowest point. The gap between the ramps should equal the spine thickness of the book. Tape or glue the three pieces together at the base. Test the cradle with the book before the adhesive sets, adjust the ramp angle if the book does not rest stably at the opening angle.

Rest the book in the cradle so the spine sits in the groove between the two ramps. The covers lie on the ramp surfaces. Never press the book open beyond the point where it rests naturally in the cradle, if the spine resists, the book needs a shallower-angle cradle, not more force.
